Yeah, yeah.

Yeah.

you?

yeah.

did you?
no, no, no.

I think I studied in the States.

I remember much.

Yeah, that's all I know about Atlanta.
It's a good place.

So it's not as happening or as good as NYC or SF, but it's still good enough that you get all the big city amenities.
And it's also much cheaper than those places.
So I feel like it strikes a happy medium if you aren't super into the pace that you see in those cities.
So it's been decent, honestly.
Yeah, nice.
Good to know.
So how's your first week been?
It's drinking from a firehose, as expected.
Yeah.
Yeah, I feel like I've just been trying to gather as much context as quickly as possible.
So my role isn't like one that gets plugged into client work directly or into...
Many parts.
It's more like a horizontal one.
So I'm going to be working across the company.
So I've just been trying to talk to as many people as possible who have like different roles.
I think I've already gotten plugged into like four different projects with like some small requests that I'm working my way through.
But lots of stuff to do.
And I think like the bulk of my work is probably going to be around how do we standardize some of the work we do, and especially the processes there.
And how do we level them up?
So, for example, I'm looking into like the content audit process we do for a lot of clients next week.
And I feel like that it can be significantly better.
And what I've realized is that every single team or every single person does it a little bit differently.
And if somebody is doing it a little bit better, it doesn't necessarily get surfaced to the rest of the teams.
So that's like the thing I'm trying to solve for.
And I feel like there's just like lots of fundamental changes we can make in terms of how we do keyword research, etc.
That is right.
Really basic at the moment.
So just trying to figure out what are these optionaries for improvements and whether some of them can be standardized enough that we actually just end up putting them into Atlas or something.
So that's been my understanding and lay off the land so far.
Yeah, nice.
I think you're working with Jason, right?
Yes, I report directly to him.
Yeah, yeah.
I haven't worked with him, but yeah, I've spoken to him quite a bit in terms of like in the earlier days, but yeah, he's pretty chill to work with.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
It's interesting because I randomly message people just to talk to them every now and then.
And Jason was one person I got in touch with because I think he was at Bardeen at that point in time and he was leading growth there.
But by the time I talked to him, he had already accepted the role at GrowthX.
So we've just been staying in touch since then.
And one of our chats, was just like, I'm opening up a new role.
Are you interested?
And I was like, sure, I can consider it.
I'm not like super thrilled about it.
I don't want to.
We're back full-time, but I'll see.
And it just progressed from there.

Because I want to be thoughtful about going full-time because I've been consulting for a couple of years.
It's going relatively well.
So I don't necessarily want to jump in if it doesn't make sense.
So, yeah.
Yeah, exactly.
Yeah.
That's kind of how I started off as well.

He found it.
I think he saw me commenting on some post or something.
So he messaged me and then I looked at the GrowthX website at that time, which was like, it was .
I mean, it was like, it was one scroll or something like that.
how small.
And I was like, okay, I'll work as a contractor and then let's see how it goes.
then, yeah, I mean, I think a couple of weeks after I joined, I was like, okay, this is pretty good, you know.
So, yeah, I moved full time and everything.
And it's changed a lot since then.
Like, back then, I think there was just like 10 of us or something in December.
And now, I don't know what it is.
It's like 70, 80 or something.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, that makes sense.
So, like, just wanted to know a bit more about the work you do and some of, like, the friction you see there.
I've, like, heard from tons of people about different things.
I'm just trying to pattern match.
So, like, it can be open-ended.
We can just dive deeper when it makes sense.
Yeah, yeah, sure.
So, my role is mostly, like, client-facing.
And it's, I think it's a bit different from the, how do I say this?
Like, all the Emmys, I think, have different skill sets.
Like, the Emmys...
Some they've hired right now, I think, are very deeply editorial.
I think me, Joe, and Ada were a bit of exceptions there.
So Ada and I joined at the same time.
And basically, we were hired essentially to build out strategies for clients.
They just needed somebody to come in and kind of build out the strategy, hopefully take charge of editorial process.
So a lot of my work is actually focused on the strategy side of things.
I have given my CMs an editorial process, and then I spend my time editing them for that checklist, so to speak.
So the content quality, you know, it's not like I would say 10 out of 10 in my book, but we're at 7 to 8 out of 10, and clients are super happy.
So it's cool.
So the biggest friction for me, I think, I don't know if you were on Marcel's call or whatever the Atlas walkthrough that he did just now, but kind of mentioned it there.
It's like...
There is a lot of friction with engineering in the sense that I don't know where they get their requirements from.
so the output that they give us, it's like they give us this kind of a tool with all these fancy buttons on it.
And I'm like, I don't understand what any of these buttons mean or what they do.
So what's the best way of operating this?
I feel like we don't get that guidance.
So, you know, they're developing these or deploying these new agentic workflows now.
And it's like there was no real instructions in terms of how to use it.
Like there was no even documentation in terms of like what is in the back end because I don't know what's going on.
So I think if there's some sort of communication on that sense, that would be great.
That is one major point of friction.
The other thing is I think you're absolutely right.
Everything is a little different in terms of how everybody does it.
Best practices don't necessarily get surfaced.
And especially with regards to, I think, LLM disability, there's a lot of experiments.
So, for example, I built a content refresh workflow, which is off Atlas, and I gave that to my CMS, and that basically rewrites the article.
It sort of chunks articles in a certain way and does all that.
I've been experimenting with putting FAQs on pages and doing that kind of stuff.
But obviously, I'm limited in the sense, like, I'm limited by the SoW.
For example, one of the things I really want to experiment with is, like, okay, what if we launch another domain?
Right?
And just have, like, I think there was an experiment that some company did.
I think was a DevTools company.
They basically wrote these articles.
They referred back to themselves.
They got a bunch of referral traffic.
Or create a microsite, maybe, within the existing site.
Like, just have, I don't want to say glossary articles, but, like, concise articles.
Right?
So, that kind of thing, you know, I'm limited by the SoW of our clients because we can't experiment.
Something that I really...
What really want to know is how do I leverage Scrunch data better?
Because Scrunch is giving us this firehose of data right now.
They themselves don't know what to do with it or present it.
And I'm finding a few opportunities.
For example, Scrunch has a nice thing where it shows where your brand is not showing up in ChatGPT, but your competitors are.
Okay, I have, and it's giving us like, I don't know, 600 prompts or something like that, right?
Okay, what do I do now, right?
So one thing I've been doing is, okay, I'm taking those questions, mapping it to existing articles, and I shove that into a refresher instructions.
But how do I know it's working?
I can't track it, right?
Because my looker tracks every single thing.
Yes, I can isolate two specific URLs, refreshed URLs, and see like, okay, referrals have increased.
But how do I know if that's for that particular query?
I don't know.
So it's like, there's this kind of, there's a lot of moving parts coming from Scrunch.
It'd be great if we had some kind of standardized way, like, okay, listen, this data, do this because of this, right?
So that would be amazing.
The other thing is, like, Scrunch reporting itself, I feel like, and I've dug into reporting quite a lot, I feel like there's no, I don't know how to use that data.
So far, the most useful sort of use case I've found for Scrunch data is we have prompt categories, and I can track my positions across those prompt categories, which is great.
But let's say my ranking drops for a particular query.
Now, I don't know why that's dropped, right?
So is there, should I be worried?
Should I not be worried?
Am I overreacting?
These kinds of questions.
So I guess with regards to your role, it's mostly, I think the intersection, there is mostly stuff that's going on in Scrunch.
You know, something else that I feel is really lacking.
To give you more context into Scrunch, don't know if Jason covered this, but Jason basically was the first to play around with it, and he designed a few prompt creation processes for Scrunch.
Now, when I replicated that process, his process worked very well for Strapi and Webflow, etc.
Strapi is one of my clients, and the prompts make sense.
But when I ported that process over to, say, Rapid or Galileo or AMP, etc., which are my other clients, the result was this bloated set of, like, 1,500 queries, which doesn't make sense.
You cannot possibly have 1,500 bottom funnel queries.
So I came up with my own sort of process to narrow that down because there may be 80 to 100 queries that really you want to track these.
This is what you absolutely want to show up for.
So what I'm saying is, like, that process needs standardization, like, best practices for which prompts to track.
I think it needs to be defined more granularly in some ways because there's a lot of judgment calls in there right now based on what's defined.
The other thing is, what is a good process?
Should I be, because we spoke to Scrunch's CSM, he was like, yeah, you want to get granular, but not too granular.
I was like, okay, what does that mean?
So I don't know what the degree is.

So, yeah, I think that's the reason why they opened up the role you're in, which is, like, all these little questions, I think the MEs and directors don't have time, and even strategists don't have time to, like, explore this because we're just busy with client work.
It'd be great to have a research function essentially answer these questions.
And, yeah, that's really it.
So, yeah.
Sorry for talking so much, but, yeah.
No, no, no, no, that's great context, honestly, exactly what I was hoping to get out of the call.
Yeah, that makes sense.
makes sense.
Yeah, I feel like, so my sense from what the conversation I've had so far is that we definitely want to do something about AEO, GE, or whatever you want to call it.
But right now there is so much low-hanging fruit, even in terms of the SEO work we do, that if we just like do that well, the quality is going to jump significantly across the board.

Because I have heard stories of like people, when they are creating strategies, they're just like literally exporting stuff from SEMrush and then just doing a judgment call, we're going to pick this, this, this.
So like they aren't even running basic clustering.
And I feel like that's such a massive misplay on the strategist's part that we really just need to fix that.
So while I definitely, I came into this role and I was thinking I would just like do a lot of stuff for LLMs.
But right now I think we just need to have like a base layer of quality when it comes to the very core.
Workflows we have, like content audits, or how do you create a strategy, how do you pick keywords, how do you pick clusters, et cetera, and so forth.
And I think there's also a pretty big gap when it comes to reporting.

I feel like the local dashboards are pretty good, honestly, but I feel like they don't really connect well, super well to the revenue part, because I think we don't do a good enough job of making clear what we need from the client when the sprint process is kicking off.

In terms of what does conversion tracking look like for a GA4, how do we want that to be changed or updated based on what we're doing, so that it actually connects to revenue, because right now we're jumping into a lot of renewals, and now we're having those conversations that we can't really connect the work we've done, even though the numbers look great, to the actual revenue part.
So I think that conversation needs to move to the start of the strategy sprint process.
So that's something that I'm looking into as well.
So, in terms of LLMs, LLMs, in terms

The way I'm thinking about approaching this, and feel free to let me know if this is a good approach or a bad one, is that I feel like a lot of people are doing some experiments with LLMs on their own.
And there are some really good nuggets in there.
So some of the work I want to do is essentially build like a centralized database of essentially what experiments are you running right now?
Just so we get like, because I don't want to reinvent the wheel and like define the processes from zero to one.
I feel like we have like enough smart people here that they're already doing something.

So I want to sort of consolidate all of that, some things they're doing into a central database and see like what are the kind of experiments we're running and maybe find a way to figure out are they actually moving the needle or not and having like a process for that too.
So like my hope is that like I create like a couple of databases.
One is for SEO workflows.
So it could be content audit, refresh, et cetera.
And then we have one for sort of like GU and AU stuff.
And then we can sort of like run sprints or anything, just like engineering does, that for the next couple of weeks, we're running this particular experiment across like all these different clients.
And I can be the one who sort of owns it and like MEs and other people can just be like support function because obviously they don't have time for this stuff.
So like when we do that a bit more systematically, I feel like we are going to figure out what are the patterns and what can we replicate on a more by just throwing more manpower at it.
Because I feel like LLMs right now, they're a pretty big black box.
We simply won't get that prompt data ever.
So we kind of have to experiment and learn and sort of feed those learnings back into the loop.
There is literally no other way to sort of become smart about this stuff in my opinion.
So that's the general direction I'm going in right now.
Yeah, that sounds pretty much one point.
I love the experimentation part.
Like even if you came and tell me like, listen, can you run this experiment on the next?
A couple of weeks, that's totally doable because it's like, it's basically inserting a few things in content the client's not necessarily going to notice and it's not going to harm them.
That would be great.
Regarding the SEO thing that you first mentioned, I totally agree.
Like, I think there's no standard benchmark for strategy creation.

And, you know, I've noticed this speaking to a lot of other clients because I've inherited a few accounts and I've noticed that the way this particular, let's say, targeting or clusters were built, it's not the way I would do it at all.
And I've had to overhaul things quite a few times.
So, like, I've had conversations with MEs where I've like, you know, we've been discussing similar topics and I've mentioned that, yeah, I begin with JTBDs and they're like, what's a JTBD?
You know, so it's like, it's a bit of a hassle.
And I think even amongst the directors, think Jacob is the only...
Everyone who gets hands-on with the strategy, et cetera, and the other directors are kind of in, kind of out.
So there's no standard way of doing things, which is a bit of a shame.
Regarding revenue, I don't know if that's a solvable problem because it's a standard content problem that exists everywhere.
What we've been doing is like, we've been doing basic  like connecting to HubSpot or whatever.
Like sometimes our clients come back with like a qualified dashboard and they're like, okay, we're hitting our ICPs, we're happy, that kind of thing.
So, yeah, it's a tricky thing.
I don't know if there's a way to standardize it, but yeah, there is a way, totally on board with that.
One of the problems, I think, on reporting that stuff is Looker itself.
Like the amount of issues that we've had with Looker is just insane.
And I haven't been able to solve any of them.
Because...
Because Luka Dash is like severely limited.
And yeah, mean, you know, ideally, we need a database and then some kind of this tool in front of it, which I think is what they're trying to build out in Atlas.
So hopefully that fixes it.
But yeah.
But yeah, it's cool that you're taking a look at SEO processes as well, because yeah, I definitely think it's needed.
Like, I know a lot of Emmys are just running like a content gap on SEMrush and then pushing that as a strategy.
And it's like, okay, you can't do that.
So yeah, good to see progress there.
Yeah.
So I think like a couple of things.
So if you could share any like internal write-up you have for running experiments, both in terms of SEO as well as GEO, anywhere you sort of like, it can be super rough.
doesn't need to be polished.
Like anywhere where you're like logging all of this stuff.
And I'm super interested.
So even just like a small voice note in terms of like what you're running so far.
think that would be super helpful.
The other thing is like the content refresh workflow you mentioned.

I would love to take a look at that and see like how different it is to how it's being run across other pods essentially and see like what's like the best version of that workflow that can be sort of distributed back to the company.
In terms of like Looker, like I've had my fair share of problems, but unfortunately, I feel like it's not really a solvable problem.
And it's still the best tool for the job because so for context, I built my own custom tool that pulls data from GSC APIs and GA4 APIs.
And I thought it would take like one month.
It instead took like six to eight months to build.
And it's still not great because like the APIs are severely limited in terms of what data they give you.
And they do give you data, especially for a large site.
You kind of just need to move to BigQuery and have like data warehousing because you literally cannot deal with that data any other way.
me, So, Thank
I feel like anything we do build, it's going to be like a approximation that we will have to tinker with.
I talked to, I think, Kirkland, and he mentioned that they are going to roll out data warehousing at some point in time.
So, like, just recording all of the data that comes in from all of our clients.
So that should give us, like, a large database that we can sort of pull against and find trends and other stuff.
But I think it's still some months away.
Yeah.
In terms of, like, conversions and reporting, I feel like we don't need to create, like, a 100% perfect solution at this point in time.
It could literally be as simple as what events are you tracking on the site right now in GA4?
What are their definitions?
And which of these are closely connected to revenue?
Or which ones of these do you care about the most?

So just, like, doing simple stuff like that and just standardizing that ask when the sprint begins, I feel like even that's going to be an improvement compared to what we're doing right now, which is just, like, stitching together stuff.
Yeah, mean, the conversions thing, that's what I thought as well.
That's one of the reasons I kind of included the conversions tab in Looker.
But the problem is most clients, like they don't, like I'll give you an example with my accounts.
Like I have Galileo, for example, they literally don't care about conversion events because they're like, we just want to show up as much as possible.
We just care only about rankings.
Okay, cool.
That's fine.
Then Strappy, Strappy cares about it and they've defined events and we track it and there's reporting on it.
We plug into HubSpot, we do that.
RAM does not give a  about conversions because we are working with a top funnel SEO team.

They're just like, okay, you know, they don't even care about our reporting, in fact, because they have their own internal dashboards and they're like, you know, we're using that, but yeah, it's cool that you're reporting this stuff.
Rapid is, you know, I think we're going to fire Rapid at some point because honestly, they're completely disorganized and yeah, like
We haven't even broached the topic of conversions with them because they have so many internal issues.
And then what else?
DataGrid.
DataGrid as well.
They don't really care about conversions or setting up anything.
They're a small team.
They don't have a marketing team.
There's nobody to set the stuff up on their team.
Their founder uses RB2B and Koala, et cetera.
So he's kind of tracking it that way, seeing like, okay, is content driving ICP?
He's happy with it.
So it's like, you know, the very, like, GA4 events, honestly, even looking at the custom requests that I get for Looker, most of them are not connected to events at all.
They're just like, you know, the people who know what they're doing are like, okay, just replicate what we're doing in Looker.
It's not necessarily connected to GA4.
They're using Qualified or Salesforce and HubSpot and all that stuff.
And then, you know, I think the biggest...
Problem is, to connect to revenue, you need somebody on their, the client needs somebody on their staff who understands how to set up an event and how to understand triggers and all that stuff.
That skill is not very wide.
It's like, there are not many marketing ops people who do that.
Like, a lot of them specialize in HubSpot and the CRMs.
They don't necessarily specialize in GA4.
So that's why I said, like, you know, standardizing that is really difficult.
And I've had, I've tried having these conversations with my clients, but, you know, it's like, they literally don't care.
They're like, they either don't care or they don't have resourcing.
So, yeah, that's, I'd say that's the main challenge.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I think like, what you say makes complete sense.
And I feel like when I talk about standardizing, I definitely don't want to roll it out and push it on clients unnecessarily.
So I think that can probably be a judgment call when we're actually closing the account in terms of like, what KPIs do they actually care about?
And it's more about like, if they care about revenue, then.
We can make that ask, and if they don't do it, then we can go back and say, we already told you to do it like six months ago, it's still not done, hence we can't do it.
But it's our fault.
I know, but I feel like it makes the renewal conversation just a little bit easier.
Yeah, yeah.
We're proactive, and we followed up, and you didn't do it.
Like, it puts the ball in the other court.
So, like, definitely don't want to roll it out across all clients, especially if they don't care about it.
I think that's fine.

Like, while we're talking about Looker, do you feel like there's anything missing there that you'd have to do on a recurrent basis, that if we added it as a standard tab across all clients, it would be a net positive for reporting and other teams as well?
I don't think there's anything missing in terms of the data.
I think the backend update process is something I haven't been able to solve.
And honestly, I roped in a friend of mine who works with Tableau.
He's like a BI whiz.
Even he gave up.
Essentially, the problem is...
We wanted to automate Airtable to local exports.
Unfortunately, it's not possible because whenever I blend the data, the data aggregation coming from GA4 and GVSE, they're aggregated at different levels between the two bloody platforms.
And it's like you get multiple rows, the joins completely blow up, and then you don't know what data you're looking at.
So it's a manual process right now that I've given all the enemies.
They absolutely hate me for it, but I'm like, I don't have a better solution for you.
I'd say that's the biggest hurdle.
And that's why I mentioned database because I literally, I even sort of pushed like, okay, maybe we can, maybe we can push the data into DuckDB or something.
And then we'll look out on top of it, but then, you know, they're already building the analytics suite.
So I'm like, okay, it's a waste of time for me.
But yeah, I think honestly, like, you know, we need a database.
Otherwise, there's...
No way.
You can't, because we're transforming data in Looker right now, and it's a piece of .
Like, it doesn't do anything.
Yeah, yeah.
Honestly, I think realistically speaking, we aren't going to solve for that, and we just kind of have to wait for the database.
And even then, I feel like our engineering team is going to need to grow because that data is going to be crazy.
Massive.
Yeah, it's just going to be a pain in the  to manage.
So, yeah, like, I don't think that's going to get solved, unfortunately, anytime soon.

The only thing I could think of is just, like, having, I don't know if the team already has it or not, but just having, like, EAs or VAs who are, like, offshore to do that kind of manual work so your MEs can, like, don't have to wrangle with all of that stuff.
Like, that's one thing I can potentially think of doing, but other than that, I think we just have to wait for the DB.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
There's no good solution for it.
But, yeah, in terms of Looker, I'm trying to think, yeah, I mean, that's pretty much it.
The only issue I would say that I have with Looker is that...
And because client requests and goals are so different, the customization can get out of hand sometimes because everybody's tracking different things.
And they see the looker initially and they're like, oh, wow, you're tracking this stuff.
Cool.
Track these other 100 things.
And it's just me with my existing accounts.
It's on top of this, I'm handling the looker thing.
Plus there's our usual publisher, Suleiman.
He's actually a developer.
I don't know why he's doing publishing, but anyway.
So it's just the two of us.
And Suleiman has no idea how looker works.
He's like more of a backend kind of guy.
And so anytime a customization request comes, it literally takes about a week or something for me to just process where's the data coming from?
Do we even have the data?
This kind of thing.
And of course, the MEs who are logging the requests, they're not the most technically minded.
So it's like, you know, like literally we had one request where the MEs who sent over like one spreadsheet.
Which.
was like HubSpot leads.
It was just name, country, whatever stuff.
And the request was like, yeah, build us a lead scoring engine.
Build us this.
I'm like, we can't do that.
